source: Promoted from 3 real Weekly Menu Planner .docx files (residence: Earlwood, June 2026 cycle), analysed 2026-07-04. See FRAME-LEDGER.md row 4. Updated 2026-07-04 with Stuart's answers on organisation, process state, rotation, template, and roster. Updated 2026-07-05 (a) with roster volatility and per-house dinner-sharing conditionality, (b) parking the roster axis and adding the meal-option-pool + harmonization mechanism, (c) with the option-pool's real cadence (quarterly, Debbie-built, time-boxed validity, live mid-quarter edits) and the allocation modes (random / stratified random / manual override), (d) with the stratification variable (variety across the 28-day period), (e) with the review interface's concrete UI shape - a dropdown per resident x meal-slot x day cell, then (f) with the override scope (independent per-cell edits, no cascading), the artifact format (web/HTML UI), and the plan lifecycle (draft -> locked -> shopping_list_generated, terminal).
privacy_note: Documentation is internal (Stuart, 2026-07-04) - privacy anonymisation lifted for this repo. Real resident names now used. Still avoid publishing this repo externally without re-checking that footing.

organisation.name: Danny Met Sally (DMS)
organisation.note: Confirmed 2026-07-04. Explains the docx core-property creator field 'dannymetsally' seen on 2 of 3 source files - same org, not a coincidence. Nationwide, estimated 20-30 SIL homes; sticking with the residences already known (Earlwood populated, 4 others as placeholders) rather than chasing the full list for now.

current_state.process: fully manual today - Debbie hand-builds one Weekly Menu Planner .docx per resident, per 28-day period
current_state.unit_of_work: one meal plan = one resident x one 28-day (4-week) period
current_state.automation_goal: Harvest pipeline generates the same artifact per resident, so Debbie can plan across the entire fleet of residences/residents instead of one home at a time
current_state.scale_driver: revised 2026-07-05 - the primary automation value is now the meal_selection mechanism below (option pools + harmonization), which directly targets time/cost savings via shared cooking. Staff rostering is parked (see support_worker_roster) rather than pursued as the scale driver for now.

cycle.note: 28-day period, confirmed the standard unit for one resident's plan

dinner_rotation.sharing_condition: in a house where residents' dietary requirements are compatible, all residents can eat the same dinner (Earlwood's observed pattern). Where dietary requirements clash (allergens, texture modification, medical diets), a shared dinner is impracticable and residents need individual dinners instead. So 'one shared dinner per house' is NOT a company-wide rule - it's an outcome of whichever residents happen to live together, evaluated per house.
dinner_rotation.shared_across_residences: not applicable as originally framed - the real question is per-house dietary compatibility, not a nationwide policy. Deferred, not blocking.
dinner_rotation.source_evidence: identical dinner text across Earlwood's 3 residents for corresponding weeks/days, e.g. all three had the same dish on Monday of week 1 - consistent with their dietary requirements being compatible enough to share
dinner_rotation.cadence_rule: confirmed by Stuart 2026-07-04: today the rotation's cadence is arbitrary/manual ('whenever') - it is NOT a fixed real-world constraint the frame must preserve. Automation is free to set the rotation cycle however it wants; do not treat the observed 4-week/28-recipe pattern as a hard requirement.
dinner_rotation.generator_implication: the generator must decide per-house, per-period whether residents can share a dinner rotation or need individual ones, driven by each resident's dietary/constraint-block profile - not assume either way. Concrete mechanism for this: see meal_selection.allocation_process.harmonization below - overlap-detection across residents' option pools, rather than a manual judgement call.

support_worker_roster.status: PARKED, 2026-07-05 - kept as a dimension so the shape isn't lost, but fixed to a single placeholder value rather than pursued as live/volatile data right now
support_worker_roster.history: identified 2026-07-04 as support workers being rostered on/off shift by day/week/month (the 'Created with Support Worker' field and the named-CP-marker variant are roster lookups, not free text); clarified 2026-07-05 as highly volatile (staff swap at short notice). Both observations stand, but Stuart chose to park this axis rather than solve roster integration now.
support_worker_roster.revisit_when: if/when roster data source and format are found and worth pursuing - not blocking any current work

meal_selection.status: NEW mechanism, 2026-07-05 - the concrete design for how a resident's meal gets chosen, and how sharing across residents is detected rather than assumed

option_pool.scope: per resident, per meal slot (breakfast / morning_tea / lunch / afternoon_tea / dinner)
option_pool.shape: a list of up to 10 named meal options for that resident+slot, each assumed already compatible with that resident's constraint-block (dislikes, portion-guide, etc. - satisfied by construction when the pool is built, not re-checked at allocation time)
option_pool.cadence: quarterly - Debbie populates/refreshes each resident's option pools 4x per year
option_pool.validity_window: each pool version has a start date and end date it applies between - so a resident can have a sequence of pool versions over time, not one pool forever
option_pool.mutability: options can be changed mid-quarter (added/removed/altered) - a change takes effect IMMEDIATELY and stays in effect until removed or altered again. So the quarterly cadence sets the baseline refresh rhythm, but the pool is a living list within its validity window, not frozen for the whole quarter.
option_pool.generator_implication: allocation must always read the option pool version that is currently valid for the date being planned - a pool is time-boxed data (start_date, end_date, options[]), not a single static list per resident
option_pool.status: schema now includes cadence/validity/mutability; no real option pools populated yet for any resident

allocation_process.random: for each day and slot, pick one option uniformly at random from that resident's currently-valid pool
allocation_process.stratified_random: stratification variable CHOSEN 2026-07-05: variety across the 28-day period. Rather than a uniform random pick each day (which can by chance repeat the same option many times in one period), stratify allocation so that, across the 28 days, each of the resident's pool options gets used a roughly even number of times before any option repeats - e.g. work through the pool's options in shuffled rounds rather than sampling independently each day. Goal: spread usage evenly across the period, not eliminate all repetition (a 10-option pool over 28 days still repeats each option ~2-3 times, it just avoids clustering).
allocation_process.override: Debbie can override any automated allocation for any resident/day/slot if she wants to - the automated process is a default, not a constraint on her judgement
allocation_process.harmonization: smarter mode, replacing the earlier manual dinner-sharing judgement: before allocating individually, check whether an option appears in more than one resident's pool for the same house, day, and slot (using each resident's currently-valid pool version). If an overlapping option exists, assign that SAME option to every resident whose pool contains it - one cooked meal serves several residents. Residents with no shared option available fall back to random or stratified-random selection from their own pool.
allocation_process.residence_scope: harmonization operates within one residence (one house, one kitchen) - never across residences
allocation_process.priority_slot: dinner is the natural first target, matching the pattern already observed at Earlwood, but the mechanism is written to generalise to any meal slot

savings_tracking.goal: capture the time/cost saved by harmonizing, so the value of automation is measurable rather than assumed
metric_sketch: harmonized_slot_count per 28-day period - how many day+slot combinations ended up shared across 2+ residents
residents_covered_per_harmonized_slot - how many residents each shared meal served
individual_meals_avoided = sum over harmonized slots of (residents_covered - 1) - the count of separate cooks NOT needed because of harmonization
savings_tracking.status: sketch only - no calculation built, no real numbers yet

review_interface.confirmed: 2026-07-05 - the review artifact's core interaction shape
review_interface.shape: every cell in the resident x meal-slot x day grid is a DROPDOWN, populated with that resident's currently-valid option pool (up to 10 named options)
review_interface.initial_value: pre-filled by the automated allocation process (random, stratified-random, or harmonization - whichever produced that cell's pick)
review_interface.override_mechanism: Debbie reviews the pre-filled plan and changes any cell's dropdown selection directly, cell by cell, as she reviews - this IS the override mechanism described in allocation_process.override, expressed as a concrete UI control rather than an abstract capability
review_interface.override_scope: CONFIRMED 2026-07-05: Debbie can change as much or as little as she wants, cell by cell, independently. No automatic re-harmonization or cascading logic runs when she edits a cell - each dropdown is edited on its own, and whatever state she leaves the grid in when she locks it is final. This resolves the earlier open question about override x harmonization interaction: there is no special interaction: her edits simply stand, individually, as final at lock time.
review_interface.artifact_format: CONFIRMED 2026-07-05: a web/HTML UI hosts the interactive review step (resolves the earlier open question about what format supports per-cell dropdowns).

plan_lifecycle.status: NEW, 2026-07-05 - the state machine governing when a meal plan can still be edited

states.description: the algorithm has pre-filled every cell's dropdown (random / stratified-random / harmonization); Debbie is reviewing and may change any cell, as much or as little as she wants
Debbie clicks a 'Locked' button once she is satisfied with her review. Her edited state at that moment becomes final for this plan.
states.trigger: Debbie's explicit action - locking is not automatic or time-based
states.editable: UNCONFIRMED - open question: can a locked-but-not-yet-shopping-listed plan be unlocked and edited again, or does locking itself already make it immutable? See open_questions.
states.enables: a locked plan can be used to generate a shopping list

states.description: a shopping list has been created from this locked plan
states.note: CONFIRMED terminal and immutable: once a menu plan has been used to create a shopping list, it cannot be changed any further, under any circumstances

plan_lifecycle.transition_order: draft -> locked -> shopping_list_generated (strictly one-directional as far as confirmed; no reopening after shopping-list generation)

dislikes.shape: comma-separated food items, paragraph before the week heading

may_include: medical directives (weight targets, medication timing, exercise mandates)

sil_day_flag.type: per-day marker, currently embedded in the day-column header text rather than a structured field
observed_patterns: rotates week to week (different days flagged each of the 4 weeks) - Kathryn Briggs
fixed set of days every week (always Tue/Thu/Sat/Sun) - Vanessa Ryan
absent entirely (no SIL flag on any day) - Teresa

community_participation_marker.type: inline prefix within a meal cell, not a separate column
community_participation_marker.note: three different spellings/conventions across 3 residents. Given the standard-template ruling below, treat this as per-resident/drift, not three real company conventions - normalise to one vocabulary in the generator, backed by the support-worker roster.

conditional_substitution.type: prose rule embedded in a cell

running_quota.type: prose rule embedded in a cell, tracking a daily/weekly cap
running_quota.example_pattern: (only if under N for the day)

eat_out_placeholder.type: cell value delegating the meal to an outing or support worker instead of specifying food

template_ruling.implication: the two observed footer/document-control table shapes (see footer_template_variation below) are NOT two real template lineages - they are per-resident variation or drift on top of one canonical template. The generator should target ONE footer shape and treat the 1x2 variant as something to reconcile, not preserve.

open_questions: Can a 'locked' plan be unlocked and edited again before a shopping list is generated from it, or does clicking 'Locked' already make it immutable? (Only the shopping_list_generated state is confirmed as unconditionally immutable.)
How many residents typically live in one SIL home (Earlwood = 3; unconfirmed as typical) - affects how much harmonization opportunity typically exists per house
Full list of residences/resident counts nationwide - deferred, sticking with Earlwood + 4 placeholders for now
Support-worker roster data source - PARKED (single 'TBA' value in use); revisit only if/when worth pursuing
